coredata.dc:title: Visualization of salt diffusion in beef curing process using Smartphone-Assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yongqiang
Li, Chuang
Li, Wenting
Hu, Xuetao
Zhang, Xinai
Li, Zhihua
Huang, Xiaowei
Zhang, Huijuan
Shi, Jiyong
Zou, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensor was proposed for real-time fluorescence imaging and monitoring of salt diffusion in beef curing process. The glutathione modified carbon quantum dots @ curcumin (CQDs@CCM) probe with dual-emissive properties was prepared using natural biomass materials. In the probe, the CQDs with blue fluorescence was quenched by CCM due to the inner filter effect (IFE). When the probe was exposed to highly saturated sodium salt, CCM formed a complex with sodium ions, interrupting the IFE behavior and resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CQDs. Significantly, the innovative application of CCM in curing scenarios effectively circumvents the interference of other metal ions. To our knowledge, this is the first report to apply smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensor to salt imaging of cured beef. Notably, the finite element analysis was performed to simulate the diffusion of salt in beef using COMSOL, and the simulation showed similar results to fluorescence analysis, indicating the established visualizing method and the simulated model can provide effective information for beef marination process. This study provide technical support for precise control of the beef curing degree and offer new insights into the fluorescence imaging in food flavor monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salt diffusion
Beef curing
Fluorescence detection
Visualization
Simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sodium ion detection using prepared fluorescent probes
Visualization of sodium salts in cured beef
Morphology and chemical structure characterization of prepared probes
Optical property characterization of prepared probes
Analytical performances of developed probe to sodium ions
Fluorescence visualization of salt distribution in cured beef

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM image of CQDs; (c) Particle size distribution map of CQDs; (d) FTIR spectra of the prepared materials.

(a) The effect of CCM on the fluorescence properties of CQDs; (b) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs and absorption spectra of CCM; (c) Fluorescence lifetime curves of CQDs before and after adding CCM; (d) The effect of sodium ions on the fluorescence properties of the prepared composite probe which dosage ratio of CQDs and CCM is 1:2.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the composite probe exposed to different concentrations of Na+ ions (0 to 600 mM); (b) UV absorption spectra of composite probes before and after the addition of Na+ ions; (c) The dependence of response values of the probe on Na+ ions concentration; (d) Dependences of the performance of the probe on storage time (0–15 days).

(a) Fluorescence images of the probe exposed to different concentrations of Na+ ions (0 to 600 mM); (b) The dependence of color response values (C/Y) of the probe on Na+ ions concentration; (c) Salt detection APP in cured beef based on smartphone and OpenCV image processing library.
Schematic diagram of the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for visualization of salt diffusion in cured beef.

head.ce:title: Visualization of salt diffusion in beef curing process using Smartphone-Assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of Intelligent Agriculture and Agri-products Processing, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensor was proposed for real-time fluorescence imaging and monitoring of salt diffusion in beef curing process. The glutathione modified carbon quantum dots @ curcumin (CQDs@CCM) probe with dual-emissive properties was prepared using natural biomass materials. In the probe, the CQDs with blue fluorescence was quenched by CCM due to the inner filter effect (IFE). When the probe was exposed to highly saturated sodium salt, CCM formed a complex with sodium ions, interrupting the IFE behavior and resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CQDs. Significantly, the innovative application of CCM in curing scenarios effectively circumvents the interference of other metal ions. To our knowledge, this is the first report to apply smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensor to salt imaging of cured beef. Notably, the finite element analysis was performed to simulate the diffusion of salt in beef using COMSOL, and the simulation showed similar results to fluorescence analysis, indicating the established visualizing method and the simulated model can provide effective information for beef marination process. This study provide technical support for precise control of the beef curing degree and offer new insights into the fluorescence imaging in food flavor monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curing is a common processing method for beef products, where beef is soaked in a saltwater solution to improve its flavor and quality. Cured beef is highly favored by consumers due to its unique taste and nutritional value [1]. In the industrial production process, the diffusion of salt during curing is influenced by various factors such as salt concentration, temperature, and direction of muscle fibers, all of which affect the uniformity and depth of salt diffusion [2]. The evenness and depth of salt diffusion directly impact the flavor, quality, and food safety of cured beef products [3]. Therefore, accurately characterizing the salt diffusion and distribution of beef during the curing process is crucial for improving the quality of cured beef and ensuring food safety.

Traditional chemical metrology method has been used to track salt diffusion in cured meat by measuring the changes in sodium chloride (NaCl) content in specific regions of the meat [4]. This method has drawbacks such as complex procedures, sample destruction, and time-consuming processes. With the advancement of emerging technologies, researchers have proposed the application of techniques such as nuclear magnetic resonance, computed tomography, and impedance measurement for detecting salt content during the meat curing process [5–7]. However, these techniques rely on expensive instrumentation or are not capable of rapid imaging [8], which hinders their practical application in industrial production. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop new rapid, accurate, and cost-effective methods for salt diffusion detection and imaging.

Fluorescence spectroscopy and imaging techniques can utilize the color change of fluorescence to achieve real-time fluorescence imaging of target ions within biological organisms [9]. This technology has advantages such as high sensitivity, simplicity, rapidity, and low cost, which have attracted wide attention in fields such as life science and food analysis [10]. It is expected to be applied to trace analysis of salt content in cured beef. Traditional organic-based or enzyme- and nucleic acid-based fluorescent probes used for sodium ion detection have limitations such as complex preparation, poor stability, and environmental hazards [11,12], which are not suitable for the detection scenario of cured beef. Therefore, under the premise of not affecting beef quality and food safety, how to achieve effective and stable visualization of salt content during the process of beef curing is the key scientific problem.

Natural biomass-based fluorescent probes provide possibilities for designing harmless probes to address the aforementioned problem. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared using readily available natural biomass are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials [13]. The construction of fluorescent probes for sensing using functionalized CQDs is a current research hotspot [14,15]. Curcumin (CCM), derived from turmeric, is another natural polyphenolic compound that has been widely used as a food additive. CCM exhibits excellent fluorescent characteristics, and its molecule contains a β-diketone structure that can chelate with many metal ions [16]. This non-specific nature makes it difficult to be applied to the detection of specific metal ions, limiting its application in complex matrices.

Inspired by the above research, we envision the application of CQDs and CCM in the limited scenario of beef curing might be a viable strategy. In cured beef, saturated salts distributed in the interstitial spaces of beef muscle can effectively avoid interference from other metal ions on the fluorescence signals of CQDs@CCM probe. Sodium ions dominantly chelate with CCM and quench the fluorescence of CCM due to the inner filter effect (IFE), further triggering the sensing response of the prepared sensor. The proposed probe with harmless and low-cost merits holds great promise for determination and visualization of salt content during the process of marinating beef with a smartphone-based portable device (Scheme 1 ). The specific sensing mechanism was explored in detail in the work.

The fresh beef meat was purchased from local supermarket in Zhenjiang, China. The sodium chloride used as a table salt was purchased from Baifu Food Additives Co., Ltd. in Henan, China. Curcumin and glutathione were provided by Shanghai National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents used were of analytical grade, and the water used was ultrapure water (18 MΩ/cm).

The preparation of probes was carried out using BPG-9106B blast drying oven (Yiheng technology, Shanghai, China). The morphology and structure of probes were characterized using JEM-1230 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The chemical structure was analyzed using Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (ThermoFisher, Massachusetts, USA). The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using F98 spectrometer (Lengguang technology, Shanghai, China) and UV-1601 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The zeta potential and particle size were available using the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern, Malvern, UK). Fluorescence lifetime measurement were taken using the Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics, Hamamatsu, Japan). Fluorescence imaging was carried out using CM-10A UV fluorescence imaging box (Spectroline, USA).

In order to prepare a non-toxic fluorescent probe, this study chose glutathione (GSH) and curcumin as the initial materials. Firstly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method [17]. To avoid the use of other harmful reagents, the synthesis of CQDs was simplified as follows: 50 mg of GSH was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. After complete dissolution, the solution was transferred to a sealed reaction vessel and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. The obtained solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 12 h to obtain purified CQDs. The purified CQDs were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

Traditional probes utilize a single fluorescence peak signal as the output to construct detection models, which are susceptible to interference from environmental fluctuations. Ratiometric fluorescent probe with dual signal peaks, utilizing one of the signal peaks as the reference signal, can effectively avoid the adverse effects of environmental fluctuations on the detection results. In this work, CQDs and CCM were used to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes. To simplify the experimental steps, the prepared CQDs were mixed with curcumin (CCM) in a certain proportion, stirred, and incubated for 3.5 min to obtain the composite probe (CQDs@CCM). Finally, the obtained probe solution was stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sodium ion detection using prepared fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: 500 μL of CQDs@CCM solution was added to 500 μL of ultrapure water. Then 500 μL of low-sodium substitute salt and sodium chloride solution of different concentrations were added to the above solution, followed by stir and incubation for 6 min. The fluorescence spectrum of the probe using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength set at 330 nm were measured and recorded. Finally, a standard curve for the assessment of low-sodium substitute salt and sodium chloride content was established based on the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I540/I420) of CQDs and CCM at 540 nm and 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of sodium salts in cured beef
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of beef marinated with low-sodium substitute salt and sodium chloride were processed based on previous work [18]. As shown in Fig. S1, three similar beef legs were selected as experimental and replicate samples, and each beef leg sample was divided into two parts, then each part was cut into six pieces for studying the effect of different marinating times of 0, 60, 120, 180, 240 and 300 min on salt diffusion in beef. That is, there were three duplicate samples for each piece of beef samples. Importantly, the two parts per replicate were marinated in low-sodium and sodium salt marinade respectively, and six pieces per treatment per replicate were marinated separately from the next six pieces for the same treatment and next replicate. That is, six portions of the pickling solution were prepared for curing the samples mentioned above (Fig. S1). Five surfaces of each slice of beef were sealed using paraffin wax and waterproof tape, ensuring that only one side came into contact with the marinating solution. Subsequently, the probe solution was uniformly sprayed onto the surface of the marinated beef using a sprayer. After a 4-minute reaction period, the beef samples sprayed with the probe solution were placed into an ultraviolet (UV) fluorescence-imaging chamber. The fluorescent characteristics of the beef samples were observed, and the distribution of salt in the beef samples was visualized.

Pickling samples for 0, 60, 120, 240 min of sodium chloride salt curing were selected to explore the applicability of probes. The established method was firstly used to evaluate the sodium chloride content in cured beef, and then the results were compared with standard chemometrics method [19]. The non-parametric test in SPSS (version 21.0) software was used to compare the differences between three batches of beef samples, and the Kruskal-Wallis H test, median test and Jonckheere-Terpstra test were used to analyze whether there were significant differences between the three batches of samples [20]. Furthermore, two factor analysis of variance was used to analyze salt content in beef. Using the general linear model program, the salt content data in beef samples were analyzed with the salting type (low-salt and sodium chloride curing) and salting time as fixed factors and beef samples as random factors. The model included single factor effects and the interactions between salting time * salting type.

The finite element analysis simulation was performed using COMSOL Multiphysics 6.0 software, mainly using the dilute material transport module. During the simulation process, it was assumed that the beef sample was regular, the diffusion coefficient was constant, and the NaCl content in the beef was zero. Specific parameter settings can refer to previous work of our team [18]. The obtained data was processed using Origin 2021 software, and a detection model was established using nonlinear curve fitting. Based on Android programming and OpenCV 3.2 library functions, the obtained fluorescence image data was preprocessed, segmented and color extracted, and quantitative detection model was finally established for rapid prediction of salt content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure characterization of prepared probes
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, GSH was employed for synthesizing CQDs with blue fluorescence emission using hydrothermal method. The reasons for selecting GSH as the carbon source for CQDs preparation are as follows: firstly, GSH is rich in γ-glutamyl and cysteine functional groups, which provide active sites for intermolecular interactions; secondly, GSH is non-toxic, easily obtainable, and possesses antioxidant effects [21], which can synergistically protect beef quality with curcumin. TEM was used to characterize the morphology of the synthesized CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The CQDs exhibited a circular shape, with uniform dispersion and no apparent aggregation. Further characterization using high-resolution TEM revealed that only some quantum dots had distinguishable lattice fringes (Fig. 1 (b)), indicating the presence of both crystalline and amorphous carbon forms in the prepared CQDs [22]. The size distribution histogram of CQDs showed that their sizes were smaller than 10 nm, with an average size of 4.34 nm (Fig. 1 (c)). The above characterizations demonstrate the successful synthesis of CQDs in this work, laying the foundation for exploring the quantum confinement effect of CQDs and their optical interaction with CCM.

The prepared probe was characterized for its chemical structure using FTIR spectroscopy. According to the results shown in Fig. 1 (d), the infrared peak at 882 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C-H bond in the benzene ring. The peaks at 1057 cm−1, 1394 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, 2950 cm−1, and 3629 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of -C-OH, and the functional groups of -C-N, -C = O, -C-H, and -O-H, respectively[23,24]. By analyzing the infrared spectral peaks of different probes, it can be observed that the composite probe of CQDs@CCM exhibits the main infrared peaks of both CQDs and CCM, indicating that the combination of the two does not significantly affect their chemical structures. It is worth noting that when sodium ions are introduced into the CQDs@CCM probe, the infrared peak intensity at 1057 cm−1 significantly increases. This suggests that the introduction of sodium ions alters the binding mode of oxygen-containing groups in the probe, thereby supporting the chelation between CCM and metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property characterization of prepared probes
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CCM and the excitation wavelength was studied. As shown in Fig. S2 (a), the fluorescence emission of CQDs exhibits a red shift as the excitation wavelength varies from 300 nm to 450 nm, accompanied by significant changes in fluorescence intensity. This phenomenon is similar to the results reported in previous literature [25]. The carbonization process of GSH leads to changes in the molecular structure and charge distribution of CQDs, resulting in the formation of energy level traps on the surface of CQDs. Intramolecular transitions occur when the CQDs molecules are excited, leading to changes in the relative energy levels and electronic transition wavelengths between the excited state and the fluorescent state, resulting in a red shift of the fluorescence peak of CQDs [26]. The strong fluorescence intensity of CQDs is mainly attributed to the abundant amino groups contained in GSH. Previous study has shown that increasing the introduction of amino groups can promote direct band-to-band recombination of electron-hole pairs, reduce the band curvature, and thereby improve the luminescent efficiency of CQDs [27]. The corresponding contour plots show that the optimal excitation wavelength (Ex) for the synthesized CQDs in this work is 360 nm, at which the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum at an emission wavelength (Em) of 440 nm (Fig. S2 (b)).

Compared with CQDs, the fluorescence emission peak position of CCM only undergoes slight variations within the excitation wavelength range of 280–420 nm (Fig. S2 (c)), indicating that the molecular structure and energy level state of CCM are not affected by the excitation photons, and the stable molecular structure of CCM plays a decisive role in its fluorescence peak position. The corresponding contour plot shows that when the excitation wavelength is 320 nm, the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum at 560 nm (Fig. S2 (d)). Taking into account the fluorescence properties of both CQDs and CCM, especially the relatively fixed emission peak position of CCM, this work adopts 330 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength for subsequent studies. Under this condition, the fluorescence peak positions of CQDs and CCM are located at 420 nm and 540 nm, respectively.

Further investigations were conducted on the influence of CCM on the fluorescence properties of CQDs. Firstly, the dosage of CQDs was fixed at 400 μL (0.5 mg/mL), and different volumes of CCM solution (0.5 mg/mL) were added to study the interaction between CCM and CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), with the increase in the dosage of CCM from 0 to 800 μL, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 420 nm gradually decreased, while the fluorescence intensity of CCM at 540 nm increased gradually. When the dosage of CCM reached 800 μL, the fluorescence of CQDs was completely quenched. The explanations for fluorescence quenching usually involve mechanisms such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electronic transfer (PET) [28]. Among them, IFE refers to the phenomenon where the acceptor absorbs the excitation light or the emission light of the donor, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the donor. Both IFE and FRET require overlapping of the fluorescence spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor [29]. The difference lies in the fact that FRET requires the distance between the donor and the acceptor to be close enough (<10 nm) [30], while IFE does not have this requirement. Additionally, FRET and IFE correspond to dynamic quenching and static quenching, respectively [31].

In order to reveal the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CCM on CQDs, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CCM were studied. Fig. 2 (b) shows that the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of CCM, indicating the possibility of FRET or IFE behavior. Zeta potential measurement revealed that the Zeta potentials of CQDs and CCM were −14.8 mV and 22.5 mV, respectively. This result indicates that they possess opposite charges, implying that the distance between CQDs and CCM cannot satisfy the requirements for FRET. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs and CQDs@CCM were measured, and the results in Fig. 2 (c) show no significant difference in the fluorescence lifetimes between CQDs and CQDs@CCM. It is generally believed that dynamic quenching corresponds to a shortened fluorescence lifetime, while static quenching corresponds to unchanged fluorescence lifetime [32]. The above results suggest that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by CCM is triggered by the IFE behavior.

Through above optimization research, it was found that when the ratio of CQDs to CCM is 1:2, CCM can completely quench the fluorescence of CQDs. The influence of sodium ions on the fluorescent probe under this condition was further investigated. Experimental results showed that with the increase of sodium ion dosage, the fluorescence peak of CCM at 540 nm gradually decreased, while the fluorescence peak of CQDs did not recover (Fig. 2 (d)). This may be because the dosage of CCM is too high, resulting in a large amount of CCM still present in the solution, making it difficult for the fluorescence of CQDs to recover, contradicting our assumption of a ratiometric fluorescent probe. Therefore, the optimal strategy is not to have a mass ratio of 1:2 between CQDs and CCM, but to choose a mass ratio of 1:1, which can retain the fluorescence signals of both CQDs and CCM, and is beneficial for constructing a more stable fluorescent probe. It is worth noting that when sodium ions are added to the fluorescent probe at a mass ratio of 1:1, the fluorescence signal of CQDs shows a slight increasing trend (Fig. S3), indicating that sodium ions trigger the complexation between CCM and metal ions, partially relieving the IFE behavior and resulting in the recovery of fluorescence intensity of CQDs. In addition to the changes in fluorescence spectra, a change in the fluorescence color of the probe from yellow to blue was also observed (Fig. S3), indicating the potential of the probe for sodium ion fluorescence visualization. Based on these phenomena, the prepared probe has great potential for fluorescence imaging of sodium ions.

In order to achieve the best analytical performance of the probe, the effects of incubation time, pH, and reaction time on the sensing performance of the probe were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4 (a), with the prolongation of incubation time, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CCM decreased and increased respectively, leading to an increase in the response signal of the probe (the ratio of fluorescence intensity between CCM and CQDs, denoted as I540/I420). After incubation for 3.5 min, the fluorescence intensity reached a steady state, so 3.5 min is selected as the optimal scheme for preparing the probe. Due to the sensitivity of CCM and GSH to pH, the effect of pH on the composite probe was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4 (b), within the pH range of 6.5–8.0, the probe exhibited slight signal fluctuations, meanwhile the probe signal was significantly affected beyond this range, indicating that the probe is more suitable for neutral detection environments. The reaction time of the probe for fluorescence spectrum detection and colorimetric detection of 500 mmol/L sodium ions was studied respectively. For fluorescence spectrum detection, with the extension of reaction time, the response value decreases initially and then reaches a dynamic equilibrium at 6 min (Fig. S4 (c)). For colorimetric detection, the obtained image is first converted from the “RGB” space to the “CMYK” space, and the change in fluorescence color is described by monitoring the ratio of “Y” to “C” value (Fig. S4 (d)). The results show that after 4 min of reaction, the fluorescence color remains basically unchanged, indicating that the spectral detection method is more sensitive compared to the colorimetric method. Based on the above results, the reaction times for spectral detection and colorimetric detection are optimized to 6 min and 4 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of developed probe to sodium ions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectral response of the CQDs@CCM probe to different concentrations of sodium ions was studied under optimized experimental conditions. The results showed that as the concentration of sodium ions increased from 0 to 600 mmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of CCM at 540 nm gradually decreased while that of CQDs at 420 nm slightly increased (Fig. 3 (a)). This is because the formation of a complex between sodium ions and CCM leads to the fluorescence quenching of CCM and inhibits the IFE between CCM and CQDs, resulting in partial recovery of CQDs fluorescence. However, it should be noted that the fluorescence of CQDs cannot be fully restored.

To further explain this phenomenon, the UV absorption spectra before and after the addition of sodium ions were analyzed. The results showed that although the addition of sodium ions led to a decrease in the UV absorption peak of CCM, there still existed a significant UV absorption peak near 430 nm (Fig. 3 (b)). This indicates that the complex of CCM and sodium ions still has a certain inhibitory effect on the fluorescence of CQDs, resulting in the incomplete fluorescence recovery of CQDs. Fig. 3 (c) shows the relationship between the response value (I540/I420) of the composite probe and the concentration of sodium ions in the range of 0 to 600 mmol/L. The regression equation can be obtained as follows: Y = -0.002X + 1.611 (R2 = 0.996). This equation can be used to detect sodium ions in the range of 50 to 600 mmol/L, and the concentration at which the limit of detection (LOD) was determined was found to be 16.27 mmol/L using a triple Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3) approach.

The stability of the composite probe was tested for a duration of 15 days. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the composite probe slightly decreased starting from the third day but gradually stabilized after 7 days (Fig. 3 (d)). After 15 days of storage, the fluorescence response value of the composite probe only exhibited a 3.18 % attenuation compared to the initial value, indicating that the probe has good stability. Furthermore, a cured beef sample was partitioned into four distinct portions, and the reliability of the proposed probe was assessed by employing the tritation-based analysis method recommended by the Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC) [19] and the established method for sodium chloride detection. The results in Table S1 showed there was no significant difference (P > 0.05) between the proposed method and the tritation-based analysis, indicating that the prepared probe has great reliability in detecting sodium chloride in cured beef.

Statistical analysis was carried out on the obtained sample data. Multiple independent sample tests were conducted on beef samples before pickling to analyze beef variations between different batches. From Tables S2 and S3, it can be seen that there are 8 data points in each batch, with rank means of 12.00, 12.88 and 12.63, and a progressive significance of 0.967, which is greater than 0.05, indicating that there is no significant difference between the three batches of samples. The median test and Jonckheere-Terpstra test were also conducted, and the results showed progressive significance of 1.000 and 0.851, respectively (Tables S4, S5 and S6). The results from the three tests were consistent, suggesting that there is no significant difference between the three batches of beef samples. Furthermore, the influence of salting time and salting type on the marinating effect was explored using two-factor analysis of variance. From the results in Table S7, the significance of salting time, salting type and the interaction between the two factors was 0.000, which was less than the significance level of 0.05, indicating that all three behaviors had a significant impact on the salt content of beef. In addition, Table S8 shows the multiple comparison results of salting time, which shows significant differences in the marinating effect of different salting times. This indicates that the three factors have significant effects on the salt content of beef.

The performance of the fluorescence colorimetric detection of sodium ions using the proposed probe has also been investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), as the concentration of sodium ions increases, the fluorescence color of the probe gradually changes from yellow to blue. By converting the captured image to CMYK mode, the colors of the ‘C’ and ‘Y’ channels can be obtained, and the contrast between the two channels exhibits a gradual increase and decrease, indicating the ratio of ‘C’ and ‘Y’ values can be used to establish the detection model. Fig. 4 (b) shows the relationship between the sodium ion concentration and the response value (C/Y), resulting in a standard curve of Y = 0.090*exp(0.009X) + 0.298 (R2 = 0.999), indicating the good detection accuracy of the method. On this basis, a beef salt detection APP was developed based on smartphone using the OpenCV image processing library (Fig. 4 (c)). By selecting the region of interest, the probe fluorescence color information can be calculated in real time, and the established mathematical model can be used to achieve rapid prediction of the salt content of marinated beef.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence visualization of salt distribution in cured beef
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the introduction section, although a quantitative detection model for sodium ions has been established in this work, the constructed probe seems difficult to be applied in complex scenarios due to the non-specific metal complexation effect of CCM. To address this issue, this study innovatively proposes the application of the composite probe in the monitoring of salt content distribution in cured beef. The advantages of this method are as follows: Firstly, a fluorescence probe is prepared using natural substances, which is non-toxic and eliminates concerns about beef safety during application. The abundant active groups on the surface of the composite probe also exhibit good antioxidant properties. Additionally, the green preparation process avoids environmental pollution. Secondly, the application scenario of the fluorescence probe is innovative, cleverly utilizing the chelation effect between CCM and metal ions to induce fluorescence changes. In this study, due to the specific limitations of the beef curing process, where Na+ dominate the content and show an aggregated distribution, interference from other metal ions can be avoided. Therefore, the prepared fluorescence probe demonstrates substantial value in studying the curing process.

This study first used low-sodium alternative salt and sodium chloride to marinate beef under atmospheric pressure. A composite probe was sprayed onto the surface of the marinated beef. When the yellow fluorescent CCM combined with CQDs with blue fluorescence properties, the fluorescence of CQDs at 420 nm was suppressed by CCM through the IFE behavior. When the probe solution was exposed to beef samples, the CCM in the probe chelated with excessive salt, causing the fluorescence quenching of CCM itself while the suppressed blue fluorescence partially recovers due to the termination of IFE behavior. The fluorescence visualization results of sodium salt distribution in beef are shown in Fig. S5. The yellow fluorescence almost disappeared on the side close to the marinating salt, indicating that the marinating liquid had completely penetrated. In the interior of the beef away from the marinating liquid, the yellow fluorescence was stronger, indicating lower concentrations of sodium ions could not suppress the yellow fluorescence of CCM. Therefore, the changes in fluorescence color can be used to determine the distribution of sodium salt in beef, achieving visual monitoring of the beef marination process.

The experiments also found that under the same marination time, the area of enhanced blue fluorescence in the beef samples marinated with low-sodium alternative salt was smaller compared to those marinated with sodium chloride, indicating slower marination rate and shallower marination depth using low-sodium alternative salt. Furthermore, the diffusion process of marination solution in beef was simulated using COMSOL 6.0 software, and the parameter settings of the simulation model can be obtained from our previous work [18]. The left interface of the model was selected as the inflow surface of salt in beef, and set the other surfaces as non-flux for unidirectional diffusion research. The simulation results were shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7, The linear color bars in the legend represent the salt concentration in beef. The trends and differences in the diffusion of low sodium substitute salts and sodium chloride in beef over time can be clearly seen from the 3D surface map and 3D streamline map. The simulated results are consistent with the fluorescence imaging results, indicating that the established method of visualizing the beef marination process and the simulated model can provide effective information for beef marination process. More importantly, this method can also be extended to other marination scenarios, which is of significant importance for revealing the marination process and mechanism, and improving marination efficiency.

In this work, aiming to address the lack of economical, practical, and efficient visualization methods for beef marination processes, a ratiometric fluorescent probe (CQDs@CCM) using GSH and CCM as synthetic materials was prepared to study the distribution of salt in beef during the marination process. CCM can suppress the fluorescence of CQDs through IFE behavior. When the probe is exposed to sodium salt, CCM can complex with them, quenching its own spontaneous fluorescence and partially relieving the IFE behavior, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CQDs. However, this method lacks specificity towards sodium ions and has difficulty achieving good detection results in complex matrices. Furthermore, this work innovatively applied the probe to the study of salt diffusion of beef marination. Under this condition, the sodium ion content is highly saturated and dominates the changes in fluorescence signals of the probe. Finally, the visualization of the salt distribution during the beef marination process was successfully achieved by utilizing the prepared fluorescent probe. This study established a high-precision and highly stable salt content monitoring and prediction model for beef marination, providing technical support for precise regulation of the beef marination process.

Yongqiang Shi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Chuang Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenting Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Xuetao Hu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhihua Li: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xiaowei Huang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Huijuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jiyong Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110382.

ce:section.ce:para:
